label: March 2026 · Grid collapse
description: Cuba's National Electric System collapsed on March 16, 2026. Did Cuban prefixes disappear from BGP?
narrative:
When Cuba's national power grid collapsed in March 2026, Internet traffic dropped sharply — Cloudflare Radar reported a ~65% decline. ReachMap checked whether Cuban address space disappeared from sampled BGP collector RIBs.

It did not. All 17 BGP-observed Cuban LACNIC prefixes remained visible from all 4 sampled collector RIBs before, during, and after the event window.

This suggests the disruption was below the global routing-control plane: power, access networks, mobile service, customer equipment, or data-plane availability. ReachMap shows BGP collector RIB visibility. It does not prove end-user reachability.

BGP-visible ≠ user-reachable.
keyFinding: BGP collector RIB visibility remained stable at 4/4 across all snapshots. The routing control plane did not see the disruption.

externalSignal.label: Traffic volume
externalSignal.interpretation: User-facing Internet traffic collapsed while BGP routes remained announced. This is the classic 'disruption below BGP' signal.

layerMismatch: BGP green, user traffic disrupted. The failure was at the power, access, and data-plane layers — below the global routing control plane.
interpretationNotes: Cuba's globally announced prefix set is small, centralized, and control-plane stable.
Outages and access disruptions may occur below the global BGP layer.
This is not a failure of the tool — it is the product's core honesty: BGP visibility is one layer of the Internet, not the whole Internet.

label: May 2026 · Healthy baseline
description: Current snapshot across 5 collectors. Cuba's normal BGP visibility state.
narrative: The May 28, 2026 snapshot represents Cuba's healthy baseline BGP collector RIB visibility. All 17 BGP-observed LACNIC prefixes are visible from all 5 sampled collector RIBs across the US West, US East, and Europe. This is the reference state.
keyFinding: Uniform 5/5 consensus across all sampled collector RIBs.

label: July 2021 · Archival
description: Archival case from 2021 Havana protests. BGP remained stable during reported access-layer disruption.
narrative:
On July 11, 2021, during Havana protests, Internet Society Pulse recorded an Internet interruption from 20:00–21:00 UTC. NetBlocks documented subsequent targeted blocking of social media platforms.

ReachMap checked whether Cuban BGP prefixes were withdrawn during this window. They were not. BGP collector RIB visibility remained at 4/4–5/5 across all sampled snapshots. This archival case supports the March 2026 finding: Cuba's BGP-announced prefixes are remarkably stable.
keyFinding: Archival confirmation: BGP stayed green during a 2021 access-layer disruption.
interpretationNotes: Archival case supporting the March 2026 finding.
The July 2021 interruption involved targeted social-media blocking (DPI), not BGP route withdrawal.

label: Tonga Jan 2022 · Partial BGP
description: Volcanic eruption severed Tonga's submarine cable. BGP-observed prefixes dropped from 9 to 4.
narrative:
The Hunga Tonga volcanic eruption on January 15, 2022 severed Tonga's single submarine cable. BGP-observed Tongan prefixes dropped from 9 to 4 over ~24 hours as BGP sessions timed out. The remaining 4 prefixes stayed announced via satellite backup and international transit.

This is a real BGP-level change, but a small one. Tonga's prefix set is tiny (11 RIR records), making the visual change subtle.
keyFinding: Partial BGP withdrawal: 9 → 4 observed prefixes. Satellite/transit-backed prefixes remained visible.
interpretationNotes: Physical infrastructure failure did cause some BGP routes to disappear.
But the effect was partial and gradual — not a dramatic all-red event.
Small country, small prefix set, limited visual impact.

label: Kazakhstan Jan 2022 · Partial BGP
description: Nationwide Internet shutdown during January 2022 protests. BGP changes were real but subtle.
narrative:
Kazakhstan's January 2022 protest shutdown involved BGP withdrawal messages documented by Cloudflare. But when ReachMap parsed the RIBs, the actual BGP change was subtle: 9 prefixes dropped from BGP out of 520 (1.7%), and path families dropped ~350 (3.8%). All remaining prefixes stayed fully visible.

The overwhelming majority of Kazakh routes remained announced throughout the shutdown. Like Cuba, Kazakhstan's disruption was primarily below BGP — filtering and null-routing rather than route withdrawal.
keyFinding: Subtle BGP changes: 520→511 prefixes (-1.7%), path families -3.8%. Modern shutdowns are not primarily BGP events.
interpretationNotes: BGP withdrawal messages were real but affected a tiny fraction of prefixes.
The shutdown was primarily implemented via filtering/null-routing, not route withdrawal.
This is further evidence that modern shutdowns happen below BGP.
